close_reason: Fixed doc to say 'avoids the intermediate Vec<u8> copy that extract_path creates'
description:
secretx-core/src/lib.rs:107-110: The doc comment on extract_path_field says the implementation 'avoids an intermediate allocation: the nested object bytes are sliced from the original input with no copy, and only the target field value is placed in a Zeroizing buffer.'

This is true for the *navigation* step (json_navigate returns a sub-slice) but the very next step calls json_extract_string_field, which calls json_parse_string, which builds a String in non-Zeroizing heap memory and may reallocate several times (see avkc.1). The doc gives the impression that the allocation profile of extract_path_field is meaningfully better than extract_path(...).extract_field(...) — but the only difference is one extract_path() returning a SecretValue::new(raw.to_vec()) intermediate (one Vec) vs. zero such intermediates. The intermediate Vec is short-lived and small (the inner object bytes), so the win is cosmetic.

Fix once avkc.1 is resolved: rewrite the comment to describe what's actually saved (one Vec<u8> for the inner-object bytes; the target field is still allocated as before, now in a Zeroizing buffer).

Until avkc.1 is fixed: at minimum add a sentence acknowledging that the *target value* still goes through a non-Zeroizing String.
title: extract_path_field doc claim 'avoids an intermediate allocation' is misleading
